text.title: Normalization, Denormalization, and ER Modeling
text.summary: Normalization rules (1NF–3NF) for OLTP schemas; denormalization triggers for read-heavy workloads; ER notation and cardinality; Slowly Changing Dimension types for analytical schemas.

rule.statement: Normalize OLTP schemas to 3NF by default. BCNF and beyond are rarely needed and increase join complexity without proportional benefit.
rule.rationale: 3NF eliminates data duplication and update anomalies — the primary causes of data inconsistency in transactional systems. Over-normalization beyond 3NF creates excessive joins that hurt read performance without providing practical consistency benefits for most systems.

list.item: 1NF: atomic column values, no repeating groups, no multi-valued columns
2NF: 1NF + no partial dependencies on a composite key (every non-key column depends on the whole key)
3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependencies (non-key columns depend only on the primary key, not on other non-key columns)
BCNF: every determinant is a candidate key — stricter than 3NF, rarely required

rule.statement: Denormalize only when profiling shows that normalized queries are too slow for the SLO and the access pattern is predominantly read-heavy (>90% reads). Document every denormalization decision and its synchronization strategy.
rule.rationale: Denormalization trades storage and consistency complexity for read performance. Without measurement it creates premature complexity; without documentation it creates invisible data inconsistency risks.

list.item: Expensive join repeated on every request → store pre-joined result
Aggregate computed on every read → store calculated field
Cross-module reference needed in read path → denormalize at write time (copy field into consumer's schema)

antipattern.description: Denormalizing tables before measuring that joins are actually slow
antipattern.reason: Premature denormalization adds synchronization complexity and data inconsistency risk without proven performance benefit

list.item: One-to-Many (1:N): User —< Orders (one user, many orders)

section.title: Slowly Changing Dimensions
section.p: For analytical/warehouse schemas where dimension attribute history must be tracked.

list.item: Type 0: never change — birthdate, original account number
Type 1: overwrite — error corrections, no history needed
Type 2: add row with valid_from/valid_to/is_current — full history, most common for audit
Type 3: add previous_value column — limited history (current + one prior)
Type 4: separate history table — performance when history table is rarely queried
Type 6: hybrid (1+2+3) — current value column + full Type 2 history

section.reference: SCD Type 2 dimension table with upsert procedure
